deck.name: AI & Future Tech
deck.description: Stay ahead of the curve with vocabulary for Artificial Intelligence, machine learning, and future innovations.

cards.definition: the simulation of human intelligence processes by machines, especially computer systems
cards.context: AI is transforming how we live and work.

cards.definition: a type of AI that allows software applications to become more accurate at predicting outcomes without being explicitly programmed
cards.context: Machine learning is used for everything from recommendation engines to medical diagnosis.

cards.definition: a subset of machine learning based on artificial neural networks
cards.context: Deep learning is what makes things like image recognition and autonomous driving possible.

cards.definition: a computer system modeled on the human brain and nervous system
cards.context: Neural networks are trained on large amounts of data to recognize patterns.

cards.definition: AI that can create new content, such as text, images, or music
cards.context: Generative AI like ChatGPT has become very popular lately.

cards.definition: a type of AI trained on massive amounts of text data to understand and generate human-like text
cards.context: GPT-4 is one of the most powerful LLMs currently available.

cards.definition: the practice of designing and refining inputs for AI models to get the best possible output
cards.context: Good prompt engineering can significantly improve the results you get from an AI.

cards.definition: when an AI model generates information that is incorrect or nonsensical but appears plausible
cards.context: Be careful when using AI for research; it can sometimes have hallucinations.

cards.definition: the process of taking an already trained model and training it further on a specific dataset
cards.context: You can fine-tune an LLM to follow specific instructions or styles.

cards.definition: a branch of AI that helps computers understand, interpret, and generate human language
cards.context: The virtual assistant uses NLP to understand your voice commands.

cards.definition: the use of technology to perform tasks without human intervention
cards.context: Automation is expected to replace many repetitive jobs in the future.

cards.definition: the use of living organisms or biological systems to develop or make products
cards.context: Biotechnology is leading to breakthroughs in medicine and agriculture.

cards.definition: the branch of technology that deals with dimensions of less than 100 nanometers
cards.context: Nanotechnology could lead to incredibly small and powerful computers.

cards.definition: computing that uses quantum-mechanical phenomena like superposition and entanglement
cards.context: Quantum computing has the potential to solve problems that are currently impossible for classical computers.

cards.definition: an interactive experience where digital information is overlaid on the real world
cards.context: Pokémon GO is a famous example of an AR game.

cards.definition: a simulated experience that can be similar to or completely different from the real world
cards.context: VR headsets allow you to immerse yourself in a digital environment.

cards.definition: the network of physical objects connected to the internet, from smart fridges to industrial sensors
cards.context: IoT devices can make our homes and cities much smarter and more efficient.

cards.definition: the practice of searching through large amounts of data to find patterns and trends
cards.context: Companies use data mining to understand customer behavior better.

cards.definition: the field of study that focuses on making sure AI systems are fair, transparent, and safe
cards.context: Ethics in AI is a crucial topic as the technology becomes more widespread.

cards.definition: prejudice in favor of or against one thing, person, or group in a way considered to be unfair
cards.context: It's important to make sure AI models are trained on diverse data to avoid bias.

cards.definition: a set of rules or processes to be followed in calculations or other problem-solving operations
cards.context: The social media algorithm determines what you see in your feed.

cards.definition: the practice of using a network of remote servers hosted on the internet to store and manage data
cards.context: Cloud computing allows you to access your files from anywhere in the world.

cards.definition: the practice of protecting systems, networks, and programs from digital attacks
cards.context: Cybersecurity is more important than ever as more of our lives move online.

cards.definition: a distributed ledger technology that ensures transparency and security without the need for a central authority
cards.context: Beyond crypto, blockchain can be used for things like supply chain tracking.

cards.definition: a car or other vehicle that can drive itself without a human driver
cards.context: Self-driving cars are expected to make our roads safer in the future.

cards.definition: the branch of technology that deals with the design, construction, operation, and application of robots
cards.context: Robotics is used in manufacturing, healthcare, and exploration.

cards.definition: a hypothetical future point where technological growth becomes uncontrollable and irreversible
cards.context: Some futurists believe the singularity will happen within the next few decades.

cards.definition: an innovation that significantly alters the way consumers, industries, or businesses operate
cards.context: The iPhone was a disruptive technology that changed the mobile phone market forever.

cards.definition: the action or process of innovating (making something new)
cards.context: Continuous innovation is key for any tech company to stay competitive.

cards.definition: the ability of a system to handle a growing amount of work or its potential to be enlarged
cards.context: Our new software architecture offers excellent scalability.

cards.definition: software with source code that anyone can inspect, modify, and enhance
cards.context: Linux is the most famous example of an open-source operating system.

cards.definition: referring to something owned by an individual or company through patents or copyrights
cards.context: Unlike open-source software, proprietary software's code is secret.

cards.definition: the delay before a transfer of data begins following an instruction for its transfer
cards.context: Low latency is crucial for online gaming and real-time communication.

cards.definition: the maximum rate of data transfer across a given path
cards.context: You need a high bandwidth connection to stream 4K video.

cards.definition: the process of converting information or data into a code to prevent unauthorized access
cards.context: End-to-end encryption ensures that only you and the person you're messaging can read your texts.

cards.definition: the measurement and statistical analysis of people's physical and behavioral characteristics
cards.context: Facial recognition and fingerprint scanning are common types of biometrics.

cards.definition: technology that can be worn, such as a smartwatch or fitness tracker
cards.context: Wearable tech is becoming increasingly popular for health monitoring.

cards.definition: an urban area that uses different types of electronic methods and sensors to collect data
cards.context: Smart city technology can help optimize traffic flow and energy usage.

cards.definition: the technological side of wind, solar, and other clean energy sources
cards.context: The cost of renewables has dropped significantly thanks to new innovations.

cards.definition: a distributed computing paradigm that brings computation and data storage closer to the location where it is needed
cards.context: Edge computing can help reduce latency for IoT devices.

cards.definition: extremely large data sets that may be analyzed computationally to reveal patterns
cards.context: Big data analysis is used to predict trends and make better business decisions.

cards.definition: the process of making an algorithm more efficient or faster
cards.context: Our engineering team is focusing on algorithm optimization to reduce server costs.

cards.definition: a test of a machine's ability to exhibit intelligent behavior equivalent to that of a human
cards.context: Whether an AI can truly pass the Turing test is still a matter of debate.

cards.definition: a system or device whose internal workings are hidden or not understood
cards.context: Many advanced AI models are criticized for being a 'black box'.

cards.definition: a collection of data used to train and test machine learning models
cards.context: The quality of your result depends heavily on the quality of your dataset.
